coredata.dc:title: Insight into the novel ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 nanocomposite for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Elmorsy, Esraa S.
Amer, Wael A.
Mahrous, Ayman
Ayad, Mohamad M.
coredata.dc:description: A novel and stable composite of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized using simple solvothermal methods. The structural and morphological properties of the composite were investigated using different techniques. The ability of N-CQDs to improve the photocatalytic activity of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 for the photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) using visible and solar light was tested.The composite exhibited higher light adsorption and charge separation capabilities than N-CQDs and ZIF-8@ZIF-67. The degradation % of 32.0 ppm MB under visible light irradiation reached 94.0 % after 180 min, and the composite maintained its photoactivity after four cycles with a minimal decrease in the degradation efficiency.Scavenging tests indicated that the free electrons (superoxide radicals) played a major role in the photodegradation of MB, which is followed by hydroxyl radicals and holes. Therefore, this work provides a new, stable, environmentally friendly, and highly effective photocatalyst for the treatment of various organic pollutants under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Insight into the novel ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 nanocomposite for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under visible light irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and stable composite of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized using simple solvothermal methods. The structural and morphological properties of the composite were investigated using different techniques. The ability of N-CQDs to improve the photocatalytic activity of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 for the photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) using visible and solar light was tested.The composite exhibited higher light adsorption and charge separation capabilities than N-CQDs and ZIF-8@ZIF-67. The degradation % of 32.0 ppm MB under visible light irradiation reached 94.0 % after 180 min, and the composite maintained its photoactivity after four cycles with a minimal decrease in the degradation efficiency.Scavenging tests indicated that the free electrons (superoxide radicals) played a major role in the photodegradation of MB, which is followed by hydroxyl radicals and holes. Therefore, this work provides a new, stable, environmentally friendly, and highly effective photocatalyst for the treatment of various organic pollutants under visible light irradiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The scientific community has been faced with contamination of the environment caused by the rapid growth of global industry [1]. The presence of persistent synthetic and natural organic wastes, including pharmaceutical wastes, dyes, nitroaromatic compounds, heavy metals, and personal care products, has been causing a worldwide concern and raising researchers' attention [2]. These pollutants have carcinogenic effects and various side effects, making them very dangerous to human health and aquatic life [3]. Thus, several methods have been extensively studied for the removal of these pollutants, including reverse osmosis, electrodialysis, ion exchange, electrolysis, and adsorption [4]. However, these routes show excellent removal capacity for pollutants; most of them are not considered cost-effective. As compared to the other methods, photocatalysis is considered a cost-effective, environmentally friendly, feasible, and effective advanced oxidation process (AOP) for wastewater treatment [5]. Thus, a sustainable photocatalyst utilizing solar energy has captured researchers' attention for the last few years [6].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are defined as 0-D nanomaterials with exquisite properties, including good electrical conductivity, enhanced optical properties, and strong photoluminescence. The advantages of CQDs over metal-based quantum dots are the low toxicity and environmental friendliness [7]. As a result of their unique properties, researchers have been exploiting CQDs in photocatalysis [8], optoelectronic devices [9], bioimaging [10], and sensing [11,12]. However, nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) have been extensively studied owing to their high quantum yield (QY) [13], and increased electron density which results in a reduced band gap and extended absorption wavelength [14]. The introduction of N-CDs into monoclinic dibismuth tetraoxide was found to effectively improve the photocatalytic activity [15]. Since the separation of CQDs from wastewater is a challenging process, the photocatalytic application of supported CQDs has been extensively investigated. He et al. [16] fabricated a 3D ternary graphene-CQDs/g-C3N4 nanosheet (GA-CQDs/CNN) to improve visible light absorption and promote charge separation. The percentage of methyl orange (MO) removal reached 91.1 % using this photocatalyst. A nanocomposite of CQDs/TiO2 nanosheet (TNS) was synthesized via a facile low-temperature process as a photocatalyst for visible-light-driven Rhodamine B (RhB) degradation [17]. In addition, the TNS nanocomposite was synthesized via the hydrothermal route and exhibited an enhanced solar light-driven photocatalytic response towards lignin degradation, reaching 99.0 % [18]. CQDs decorated g-C3N4 LED-light-driven photocatalyst showed an enhanced photocatalytic degradation rate of tetracycline up to 81.9 % [19]. Furthermore, a binary material TiO2–C showed enhanced photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) and the photocatalytic activity increased 5.3 times as compared to TiO2 [20]. CQDs-embedded mesoporous α-Fe2O3 was synthesized with outstanding photocatalytic activity and the efficiency of the MB degradation reached 97.0 % using visible light [21]. In 2015, CQDs/copper tungstate (CuWO4) heterostructure was prepared, and the degradation efficiency of RhB was 88.1 % under UV irradiation [22].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) materials are highly porous crystalline materials made of metal centers and organic linkers [23]. This class of materials is distinguished by the easy control of its geometry, porosity, and size by changing the metal centers and the linkers. MOFs are also known for their unique surface area and diverse chemical properties [24]. Considering the above-mentioned properties, MOFs are considered ideal support for CQDs. The CQDs were reported to be encapsulated within MOFs pores [2]. For example, Q. Wang et al. supported CQDs on NH2-MIL-125 MOFs to be used as a photocatalyst under visible light and near-infrared irradiation. They serve as electron acceptors since they provide a nonradiative pathway for photoinduced electrons from the MOFs and prevent electron-hole recombination. Thus, the photocatalytic removal of RhB by the CQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was almost 100 % after 120 min, with 30.0 % removal through adsorption in the dark [25].

In 2002, a new subclass of MOFs emerged as zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) [26]. ZIFs are an important category of MOFs, where organic imidazole linkers are used to connect the transition metals, including zinc and cobalt [27]. Their easy synthesis techniques [28], enhanced chemical and thermal stability [29], and high porosity [30] endued ZIFs with the researchers' attention to be exploited in numerous applications, including catalysis [31], CO2 capture [32], adsorption [33], sensing [34], and batteries [35]. ZIF-8 and ZIF-67 have uniquely the same topology and organic liker; hence both materials are identical in most parameters [36]. However, ZIF-8 is exceptionally stable in aqueous media, and it has a wide band gap of about 5.00 eV, where its optimum photocatalytic performance is expressed under UV irradiation [37]. In contrast, ZIF-67 has weak stability in aqueous media and a narrow band gap of about 1.98 eV, which enables it to exhibit photocatalytic activity under visible light [38]. Fe3O4-PSS@ZIF-67 composite was used as a highly selective adsorbent toward MO in the solution with a separation efficiency up to 96.0 % [39]. Multi-metal ZIFs have also drowned the researchers' attention recently. Gu et al. [40] prepared Ag/ZIF-8@ZIF-67 core–shell composite for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol. ZIF-8@ZIF-67@POM hybrids were synthesized via a facile co-precipitation technique; the hybrids exhibited extraordinary electrocatalytic performance for the oxygen evolution reaction and long-term stability [41]. Additionally, the novel M−ZIF−8@ZIF-67 was synthesized by T. Li et al. for the effective adsorption of fipronil and its metabolites [42]. Also, ZNC/rGO derived from ZIF-8/rGO was investigated as a photocatalyst under visible light irradiation for the degradation of MB, the composite showed MB photodegradation of 98.9 % [43]. Recently, Huang et al. successfully prepared ZIF-8@ZIF-67 as a photocatalyst for dye photodegradation and hydrogen evolution. The core–shell interface enhanced the charge transfer and reduced the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole, thus providing a high charge concentration. The photocatalytic degradation of MB by the designed ZIF-8@ZIF-67 reached almost 65.0 % after 50 min under simulated sunlight irradiation [38].

Based on the above-aforementioned considerations, the combination of ZIF-8, ZIF-67, and N-CQDs for photocatalytic degradation of dyes is lacking in the literature. Hence, the present work proposed a novel photocatalyst. The photocatalyst was prepared, characterized, and used for the photodegradation of MB dye under visible light irradiation. The effect of photocatalyst dose, pH, dye concentration, and light intensity was studied. The mechanism of photocatalysis was supported by scavenger experiments that confirmed the role of active species in the degradation of MB. The reusability and regeneration ability of the photocatalyst was tested. To the best of our knowledge, the ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 photocatalyst has not been reported for wastewater treatment.

Citric acid (99.5 %) was obtained from (AppliChem, Germany). Ethylenediamine (99.0 %) and 2-methylimidazole (2-MeIM) (99.0 %) were purchased from (ACROS ORGANICS, Belgium). Cobalt chloride anhydrous (97.0 %, Alfa Aesar, Germany), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99.0 %, LANXESS., Germany), and methanol (HPLC-grade, Sigma Aldrich) were used without further purification. Hydrochloric acid (37.0 %, EMSURE, Germany) and sodium hydroxide (Fisher Chemicals, United Kingdom) were used to adjust the pH of the solutions. All solutions were prepared using deionized (DI) water.

N-CQDs were prepared according to the bottom-up hydrothermal method reported by Qu et al. with slight modifications [44]. A solution of citric acid and ethylenediamine was prepared in ultrapure water, and then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave reactor. The reactor was heated to 200 °C for 5 h, then naturally cooled to room temperature. Next, the honey-like yellowish solution was centrifuged at 14,000 rpm. Finally, the solution was subjected to dialysis to get the N-CQDs.

Polyhedron white powder was synthesized by following the procedures of Torad et al. [45]. Briefly, 315 mg 2-MeIM was dissolved in 12.0 mL methanol to form solution A, and 486 mg of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 12.0 mL methanol to form solution B. The two solutions A and B were mixed and stirred for 2 h. The stirred solution was left overnight at room temperature. The resultant ZIF-8 was obtained and purified by centrifuge then dried under vacuum for 6 h.

The photocatalyst ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was prepared similarly to the procedure described by Huang et al. with some modifications [38]. 256 mg of the previously prepared ZIF-8 were dispersed in methanol by sonication. A solution of 377 mg of CoCl2 and 25.0 mg of N-CQDs in 10.0 mL methanol was mixed with the previously dispersed ZIF-8 solution. Afterward, 170 mg of 2-MeIM dissolved in 6.00 mL methanol was added to the previous mixture and kept stirred for one day. Finally, the prepared ZIF-8@N-CQD/ZIF-67 was collected by centrifugation.

The photocatalyst ZIF-8@ZIF-67 was prepared by following the procedures described by Huang et al. with some modifications [38]. 256 mg of ZIF-8 were dispersed in methanol. A solution of 377 mg of CoCl2 dissolved in 10.0 mL methanol was mixed with the previously dispersed ZIF-8 solution. Afterwards, 170 mg of 2-MeIM dissolved in 6.00 mL methanol was added to the previous mixture and kept under stirring for one day. Eventually, the prepared ZIF-8@ZIF-67 was collected by centrifugation.

The size and the morphology of N-CQDs, ZIF-8, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 were investigated via Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEM 2100F, JEOL). The elemental composition and mapping of the prepared materials were analyzed by energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) (NEX CG EDXRF, Rigaku). Shimadzu XRD-6100 with Cu–Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) was used to study the crystal structure of the prepared materials. N2 adsorption isotherms were determined using a BEL Japan (BELSORP II mini). The calculation of BET surface area, mean pore diameter and total pore volume were done using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured using Bruker VERTEX 70v with platinum ATR model V-100. The measurements were done in the wavenumber range (500–4000 cm -1). The fluorescence of the materials was measured with Hitachi F-2700 with a 150 W Xenon lamp (Xe) as a light source. The fluorescence lifetime measuring system Horiba FluoroCube 3000U-EYM1 was used to record the fluorescence decay. UV–visible spectrometer (Shimadzu–2600) was used to determine the absorbance of the prepared materials in the wavelength range of 250–800 nm. Malvern Zetasizer Nano Series was used to measure the zeta potential (ζ) of the ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 composite. DI water was used to disperse the materials for the (ζ) measurements.

The quantum yield of N-CQDs (Fig. S1) was evaluated using quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.100 M H2SO4 with QY = 0.540 as a standard reference. Using excitation wavelength = 360 nm, the absorbance was kept below 0.100 for both N-CQDs and quinine sulfate. The calculation of the quantum yield was done according to Eq. (1), where QYs and QYref are the quantum yields of N-CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. Is and Iref represent the fluorescence intensity curves, and η is the solvent refractive index.

The QY of the N-CQDs was 84.0 % which is a high value compared to previously reported values [9,10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of the composite ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67
ce:section.ce:para:
Methylene blue was used to model the organic pollutants in wastewater to assess the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized photocatalyst. In every experiment, unless stated otherwise, 50.0 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed and sonicated with 100 mL of 32.0 ppm MB aqueous solution for 30.0 min. The mixture was agitated in the dark for 1 h to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Next, the mixture was subjected to an irradiation of a 400 W metal halide lamp (Philips MH 400 W/640 E40 CL U 1SL/6) with a light intensity of 8000 Lumine (LM), under continuous stirring, for 3 h, and the space between the light-sources and the beaker was kept constant at 10.0 cm. Every 20 min, a 1.00 mL sample was centrifugated and the UV absorbance at 664 nm was measured. The effect of photocatalyst dose, starting MB concentration, pH, light intensity, and scavenger test were done using the same procedures. The pH effect was examined by adjusting the pH value (in the range 4.00–11.0) using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH. The degradation percentage (D%) was calculated according to Eq. (2).
(2)
D % =
C 0
- C
C 0
× 100

The reproducibility of the photocatalyst was tested for MB degradation. After the first cycle, the remaining MB solution was irradiated with visible light for an extra 1 h to confirm the removal of the residuals of MB dye fragments. Then, 1.00 mL of 3.20 × 103 ppm MB solution was added to the solution and irradiated under visible light to check using the photocatalyst for the second cycle. The reusability of the photocatalyst was tested for up to 4 cycles, and the degradation % of the MB was calculated using Eq. (2).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) and FT-IR were performed to verify the crystalline structure and the chemical composition of the prepared materials. The FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs, ZIF-8, ZIF-8@ZIF-67, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF are shown in Fig. 1 a. The spectra of all materials were recorded in the range of 500–4,000 cm−1. In the spectrum of the N-CQDs, the bands at ∼ 3466 and 2933 cm−1 represent the O–H of carboxylic functional groups and the alcoholic OH, respectively. The peak at 1650 cm−1 represents the C = O stretching of the conjugated ketone [15]. While in the spectrum of the ZIF-8, the imidazole ring bending and stretching were attributed to the bands of 500–1350 cm−1 and 1350–1500 cm−1. The peaks at 3137 and 2930 cm−1 represented the C–H stretching of the aromatic and aliphatic C–H of the imidazole, respectively. The stretching vibration of the C = N group of the imidazole was identified by the band stretching at ∼ 1600 cm−1 and the C═C double bonds at ∼ 1578 cm−1. In the FTIR spectrum of ZIF-8@ZIF-67, the aromatic and aliphatic C–H stretching at 3136 and 2928 cm−1, respectively, were identical to the stretching peaks of ZIF-8. In addition, ZIF-8@ZIF-67 and the composite ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 showed similar stretching vibration of the C = N group of the imidazole that was identified by the band stretching at ∼ 1600 cm−1, and the C═C double bonds at ∼ 1578 cm−1 [46]. Notably, ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 showed no observable peaks similar to those observed in N-CQDs, which serves as evidence of the successful entrapment of N-CQDs within the pores of ZIF-67 without damaging the crystal structure [15,47].

In Fig. 1b, the XRD patterns of N-CQDs, ZIF-8, ZIF-8@ZIF-67, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 are presented. These patterns exhibited characteristic features corresponding to N-CQDs, ZIF-8, and ZIF-8@/ZIF-6 [48]. Specifically, the XRD pattern of N-CQDs showed a broad peak centered at 2θ = 24.28°, corresponding to the crystalline planes (002) of graphite-type carbon [49]. Notably, strong peaks at 2θ = 7.43, 10.47, 12.81, 14.76, 16.56, 18.08, and 24.51° were observed, which aligned well with the planes (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), and (222) of ZIF-8 [50], and were in excellent agreement with the simulated pattern of ZIF‐8 [38] Furthermore, the XRD pattern ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 showed recognizable ZIF-8@ZIF-67 peaks, suggesting that the ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 crystal structure remained intact after incorporating CQDs. Notably, the absence of distinctive diffraction peaks corresponded to CQDs in the relevant samples may be due to their low content in the composite [51]. The inset figure of Fig. 1b shows the peak at 24.28°, in the XRD of N-CQDs, corresponds to the crystalline planes (002) of graphite-type carbon. In the XRD pattern of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67, a broadness was observed at the same position revealing the incorporation of N-CQDs into the core–shell structure. The inclusion of N-CQDs in the shell of the composite might be achieved either via the formation of hydrogen bonds between the nitrogen atoms of ZIF‐67 and the –COOH groups of N-CQDs or the coordination of N-CQDs to Co2+ nodes. This confirmed the preparation of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 and the encapsulation of N-CQDs in the core–shell structure [31].

As demonstrated in Fig. 2 , the physical adsorption curves of ZIF-8, ZIF-8@ZIF-67, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 of the three materials are type I nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm indicating that all the materials were microporous with high adsorption capacity at low relative pressure. The calculated surface area and total pore volume of ZIF-8 were 1193 m2/g, and 0.521 cm3 g−1, respectively. On the other hand, the calculated surface area of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 was 1635 m2/g, and its total pore volume was about 0.696 cm3 g−1 as shown in Table 1 . The increment of the pore size from 0.521 to 0.696 cm3 g−1 coincided with extending the surface area from 1193 m2/g to 1635 m2/g with the addition of ZIF-67 as a shell to the core ZIF-8 to form ZIFF-8@ZIF-67 composite. It can be concluded that the BET surface area increased due to the core–shell assembly and the inclusion of ZIF-67 which boosted both surface area and the pore size. Furthermore, the calculated BET surface area and the total pore volume of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 increased to 1693 m2/g and 0.712 cm3 g−1 due to the inclusion of N-CQDs into the pores of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 leading to further extending both of the surface area and the pore volume. As can be seen that the photocatalyst retained higher surface (1693 m2/g) area than that of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 (1635 m2/g) [38]. This increment of the surface area and total pore volume implies the successful encapsulation of N-CQDs onto ZIF-8@ZIF-67. The average pore diameters of ZIF-8, ZIF-8@ZIF-67, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 were 1.745, 1.702, and 1.681 nm, respectively. The BET surface area and total pore volume of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 photocatalyst were large enough as an adsorbent. This property is expected to enhance the photocatalytic properties.

TEM is a very common tool to examine the size, shape, and bulk morphology of the synthesized materials. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) and elemental mapping were measured to investigate the bulk morphology and the composition of N-CQDs, ZIF-8, ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67. The HR-TEM of N-CQDs (Fig. 3 a) showed that the diameter of the synthesized dots is around 5 nm. In Fig. 3b, it is clear that ZIF-8 had a typical dodecahedron shape with sharp edges and smooth surfaces similar to previously reported work [45]. The size of ZIF-8 crystals is around 500 nm. In Fig. 3c, ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 crystals increased in size to 700 nm due to the formation of a ZIF-67 shell with a thickness of ∼ 100 nm (Fig. 3d). The SEM image and the elemental mapping of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 are shown in Fig. 3e, f, g, h, i, j, and k. EDX analysis was employed to verify the composition and the presence of the different elements revealed from the elemental mapping and to get their percentages in the synthesized ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 (Fig. 3l). C, N, O, Co, and Zn elements percentages are shown in the EDX of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-6, which proved that ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was successfully synthesized. The elemental ratio of Co:Zn was found to be 1.00:2.50, as given in the EDX analysis. Therefore, the elemental mapping and the EDX analysis confirmed the purity of the synthesized materials.

The optical properties of the synthesized materials were determined by measuring their absorbance and fluorescence emission spectra [52], Fig. 4 . The absorption spectrum of N-CQDs showed the presence of an absorbance peak with maximum intensity at 350 nm due to the π - π* transition in the UV region [53] as shown in Fig. 4a. The absorbance spectra of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 showed a broad absorbance peak at 550–620 nm. The distinct peaks could be assigned to the spin-coupled triplet transition of the Co(II) ion in the cobalt-nitrogen [38]. The measurement of fluorescence for the prepared materials is presented in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence of the N-CQDs showed the presence of an emission peak at about 450 nm that was quenched upon encapsulation into ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 composite. The prepared ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 had lower emission intensity indicating slower charge recombination; hence more free electrons were available for photocatalytic activity. The slower charge recombination rate was also proven by the decay lifetime measurement (Fig. S2), where the decay lifetime of N-CQDs, ZIF-8@ZIF-67, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was 8.997, 0.09367 and 0.7096 ns, respectively.

The band gap energy (E g) of the prepared materials; N-CQDs, ZIF-8@ZIF-67, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was evaluated using the following Tauc equation by plotting
α h ν

versus hν (eV). The E g values were obtained from the Tauc plot by extrapolating the linear part and drawing a tangent line where α = 0 as illustrated in Fig. 5
[54].

( h ν -
E g
)
where hν is the incident photon energy, K refers to the energy independent constant, and α is the absorption coefficient. In Fig. 5, the calculated band gap of N-CQDs was 3.16 eV, demonstrating that the main photoactivity of N-CQDs is in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum. On the other hand, the band gap energies, as shown in Fig. 5, of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 are 1.88 and 1.77 eV, respectively. This indicates that the photoactivity was shifted toward the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum and proves the potential use of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 as photocatalysts using visible or solar light.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 nanocomposite was examined towards the degradation of MB as a model dye. Since the synthesized composite had a wide surface area of 1693 m2/g, there is a probability of MB dye adsorption onto the surface of the nanocomposite. The removal of MB by adsorption route onto the surface of the photocatalyst was tested in the darkness for 2 h to evaluate the adsorption–desorption process accurately. As shown in Fig. S3, the maximum adsorption percentage of MB dye reached 10 % due to the surface area of the used nanocomposite. The photocatalytic performance of ZIF-8@N-CQDsZIF-67 was tested versus time under visible light irradiation. The decolorization efficiency of the nanocatalyst was studied in comparison to the previously reported ZIF-8@ZIF-67 as demonstrated in Fig. 6 a and 6b in which the absorbance of the MB spectrum decreased with increasing the irradiation time up to 180 min. Another experiment was done without using the catalyst [55] in which no change in the solution absorbance was observed for a long time (Fig. 6c). This reveals the important catalytic role of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 for MB degradation.
The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of MB dye in the presence of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was considered to follow pseudo-first-order model.

Fig. 6c shows the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs, ZIF-8@ZIF-67, and ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 for MB degradation and the photolysis of MB, which was done as a control. In addition, the degradation of MB using ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 under direct sunlight (instead of visible light) was evaluated and this sunlight-based photocatalytic activity was described in Fig. S4. Fig. 6c shows that the adsorption of MB onto ZIF-8@ZIF-67 was 30 %. However, the inclusion of N-CQDs in the shell of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 caused a decrease in MB adsorption to only 10 %. This decrease in the adsorption could be due to the pores of the ZIF-67 shell already occupied by the N-CQDs. The photocatalytic performance of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was greater than the performance of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 and N-CQDs. In the first 60 min, the degradation of MB dye reached 80 % using ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 as a photocatalyst, where it only reached 65 % and 24 % at the same time with ZIF-8@ZIF-67 and N-CQDs, respectively. Minimal degradation of MB was observed under visible light irradiation in the absence of the photocatalysts. Almost complete degradation (94 %) of MB dye occurred after 180 min using ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67.

In Fig. 7 , the kinetics of the degradation reaction were calculated [56] using Eq. (3). The photocatalytic degradation reaction of MB dye followed pseudo-first-order kinetics that was supported by the linear plot of -ln(C/C 0) vs. the reaction time with R 2 = 0.996. This indicated that the photocatalytic degradation reaction of MB dye depended only on the initial concentration of the dye [57]. The degradation reaction rate constant k = 0.0105 min−1 was obtained which indicated the activity of the synthesized ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 as a photocatalyst.

The effect of the change in the pH on the photocatalytic activity and the adsorption of MB is an important parameter to be studied. It is worth mentioning that the adsorption of MB on the surface of the photocatalyst affected the photodegradation efficiency; to an extent, a higher adsorption capacity would improve the degradation. The photocatalytic degradation of MB was carried out at different pH values with an initial catalyst dose of 0.500 mg and a dye concentration of 32.0 ppm. The pH change altered the surface charge of the photocatalyst, thus interfering with the electrostatic interactions between the MB and the photocatalyst in the aqueous media [58]. The photocatalyst ZIF-8@N-CDs/ZIF-67 was observed to be unstable at a very low pH; thus, the initial pH of the solution was adjusted to 4.00, 6.00, 7.00, 9.00, and 11.0. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the efficiency of the photocatalyst is preserved across a varied pH range from 4.00 to 11.0 exhibiting maximum efficiency at pH 7.00, where ZIF-8@N-CDs/ZIF-67 surface charge is 2.38 mV (Fig. S5). This could be attributed to the point of zero charges (pHzpc) of MB in aqueous solutions that was found to be 5.37; hence the MB dye carried a net negative charge in neutral media [59]. At pH < pHzpc, MB showed positive zeta potential, indicating that its surface was positively charged. As pH increased from acid medium to basic medium, the functional groups of MB were deprotonated, and the surface of MB became negatively charged [59]. In addition, the photocatalyst was positively charged at lower pH values [60]. Thus, at low pHs, a repulsion occurred between the photocatalyst and the MB dye leading to decreasing the efficiency of photocatalysis from 94.0 % at pH 7.00 to 64.0 % at pH 4.00. Similar results were reported [61]. However, in basic media, the photocatalyst became negatively charged, so there would be electrostatic attraction between the opposite charges of the photocatalyst surface and MB dye. This attraction was expected to enhance the adsorption of the cationic dye on the photocatalyst surface. At high pH values, the adsorption of MB on the surface of the photocatalyst was further increased to reach ∼ 21.0 % leading to blocking the catalyst's active sites [62]. Accordingly, the photodegradation efficiency of the photocatalyst decreased from 94.0 % at pH 7.00 to 88.0 % and 74.0 % at pH 9.00 and pH 11.0, respectively. Hence, the optimum photodegradation efficiency was obtained at pH 7.00, as shown in Fig. 8b [63]. Abd El Khalk et al. investigated the effect of pH on the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/ZIF-8 catalyst for MB degradation under visible-light irradiation and the MB dye removal percentage was found to increase slowly from pH 3.00 to a maximum of pH 7.00–9.00 range and then slightly decreased at pH 11.0 [64]. Also, Jing et al. [37] studied the MB degradation using ZIF-8 photocatalyst under UV light irradiation and concluded that the catalyst's performance was efficient over a wide range of pH from 2.00 to 12.0. The degradation efficiency for MB increased from pH 2.00 to pH 6.00 and then stabilized up to pH 10.0. This was followed by a high increase in degradation in a strongly alkaline environment (pH 12.0). They attributed this behavior to increased •OH radicals' concentration and the effectively adsorbed MB molecules onto the surface of the photocatalyst [37].

Initial dye concentration is an important factor that changes the degradation percentage of MB using ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 as a photocatalyst so that its influence was also evaluated (Fig. 9 a). The degradation efficiencies were studied at 8.00, 16.0, 32.0, and 48.0 ppm of MB, and the degradation percentages were 92.0 %, 94.0 %, 94.0 %, and 62.0 %, respectively. The degradation efficiency remained over 90 % after increasing the MB concentration from 10.0 to 32.0 ppm. However, the degradation efficiency highly decreased from 94.0 % to 62.0 % as the MB concentration increased from 32.0 ppm to 48.0 ppm (Fig. 9b). This decrease in the photocatalytic efficiency could be assigned to two reasons. The first one is increasing the amount of MB dye adsorbed onto the surface of the ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 photocatalyst, which could restrain the photocatalyst from light absorption for generating electron-hole pairs. The second reason is increasing the path length of the photons that enter the MB solution at high concentrations [65]. Similar results were reported by Zang et al., where the degradation efficiency decreased from 55 % to 30 % as the MB concentration increased from 10.0 ppm to 30.0 ppm, respectively [66]. Moreover, Tichapondwa et al. reported a decrease in the photocatalytic efficiency from 100 % to 25.0 % as the MB concentration increased from 2.50 ppm to 15.0 ppm [67].

In Fig. 10 a, the photocatalyst dose effect on the photodegradation is illustrated. Under pH 7, the photocatalyst doses varied from 0.100 to 1.00 g L–1, while the MB concentration, light intensity, and other experimental parameters stayed constant. The results showed that raising the photocatalyst dose from 0.100 to 0.500 g L–1 enhanced the degradation efficiency from 65.0 % to 94.0 %. This increase in the photocatalytic efficiency could be due to the increase of the photocatalyst active centers, thus, increasing the concentration of generated degradation radicals [68]. However, an additional increase in the photocatalyst dose to 1.00 g/L resulted in a minor decrease in the efficiency of the degradation. This decrease in the photocatalytic performance was attributed to the agglomeration of the photocatalyst leading to the blocking of light penetration to the active centers of the photocatalyst [66]. Buvaneswari et al. [69] reported that increasing the photocatalyst dose from 0.020 to 0.050 g/L enhanced the degradation percentage of MB from 59.0 % to 96.0 %. Nevertheless, increasing the photocatalyst dose to 0.050 g/L sharply decreased the degradation percentage of MB. Similar results were reported by Pattappan et al. [62], as the photocatalyst dose concentration increased from 0.5 g/L to 1 g/L, and the degradation efficiency increased from 78.0 % to 90.0 %. While further increasing the photocatalyst dose to 1.5 g/L, the degradation efficiency decreased to 78.0 %.

The effect of light intensity on the photocatalyst performance is illustrated in Fig. 11 a. The increase in light intensity enhanced the efficiency of MB degradation [70]. As the light intensity increased, the penetration of light to the photocatalyst active sites increased and thus increased the generation of . OH and .
O
2
-
inside the solution. Increasing the light intensity from 1.17 mW/cm2 to 3.51 mW/cm2 enhanced the degradation percentage from 55.0 % to 94.0 %, respectively, Fig. 1b. Comparable outcomes were reported by Xu et al. report [71] that showed the effect of the variation of light intensity on the photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine 6G with α-Fe2O3/diatomite composite under visible light irradiation. The degradation of Rhodamine 6G increased gradually with increasing light intensity until it reached 95.0 % at 100 W. This improvement was assigned to the increased . OH generation. Additionally, Islam et al. [72] studied the photodegradation of metalaxyl with TiO2 under solar irradiation. A gradual increase in light intensity was noticed to increase the degradation efficiency of metalaxyl. The higher degradation efficiency was attributed to the inhibition of hole-electron recombination by increasing the light intensity.

The stability and the reusability of the ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 photocatalyst was measured for five cycles, as shown in Fig. 12 . ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 photocatalyst showed only a minimal decrease in performance after five cycles. The loss of the photodegradation efficiency was 10.8 % in the fifth cycle. The slight decrease in the photocatalytic efficiency after 5 cycles is comparable to some of previously reported results [73]. This behavior indicates the high stability and the good reproducibility results of the synthesized ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 photocatalyst toward the degradation of MB dye.

To better understand the mechanism of utilizing ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 for the photodegradation of MB, scavenger experiments were performed. The reported data was normalized to 100 % in the absence of scavengers for easier representation of the effect of scavengers on the removal percent. The performance of the photocatalyst was evaluated under three different scavengers. Secondary butanol (Sec-Butanol) was used as a trapping agent of hydroxyl radical ( . OH), ethylenediamine tetraacetic disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) was used as a scavenger for holes (h+), and para benzoquinone (PBQ) was used for scavenging the superoxide radical ( . O2 –) [74–76]. As illustrated in Fig. 13 a, the presence of PBQ negatively affected the photocatalytic efficiency of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67, suggesting that the superoxide radicals were the major species responsible for the degradation of MB degradation. These superoxide radicals were formed by the combination of oxygen and photogenerated electrons. When Sec-butanol and EDTA-2Na were added, low catalytic performance was obtained, which indicated that hydroxyl radical ( . OH) and holes (h+) are active substances in the degradation process. The connection between ZIF-8, N-CQDs, and ZIF-67 caused a decrease in the band gap energy and an increase of the recombination time between the electrons and holes, thus increasing the free electrons. This improved the photocatalytic activity of the composite, as proposed in Fig. 13b.

The Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) test was performed for similar prepared MOF materials (ZIF-67, ZIF-8, and ZIF-8@ZIF-67) with spin-capturing radicals of 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrrolineN-oxide to detect reactive oxygen species including the superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals in the degradation system [38]. Under sunlight conditions, the three materials were found to produce superoxide and hydroxyl radicals but under dark conditions, no radicals were detected. These results are in accordance with the scavenger test done in our study.

Based on the scavenger test’s results, it is suggested that the reaction mechanism starts with an electron-hole separation in the three components of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 under visible light irradiation, followed by an electron transfer from the core to the shell. The catalyst's surface, characterized by a high electron density, interacts with oxygen molecules (O2), resulting in the formation of superoxide radicals (.O2 –). Additionally, the hole can extract electrons from the hydroxyl ions to produce hydroxyl radicals (HO⋅). After that, the produced.O2– and HO⋅ can effectively degrade MB. The photocatalytic reaction mechanism could be illustrated as follows:
ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 + hν → (h+ + e-)
O2 + e-→ . O2 -
H2O ↔ H+ + OH–
OH– + h+→ . OH
O2
. O2 - + H++ 2e-→ H2
H2O2 + e-→ . OH + OH–
MB + . O2 - / . OH → Degradation products

Upon irradiation in the presence of the synthesized photocatalyst, the MB dye molecules were broken into small fragments that can undergo complete conversion to CO2, and inorganic ions like ammonium, nitrate, and sulfate upon irradiation for a long time. The degradation products were estimated in similar studies for photodegradation of MB by utilizing MOFs, like ZIF-8 and ZIF-11-based composite [37,77] as photocatalysts. Further research about the intermediates and the final degradation products and their toxicity shall be carried out using different techniques, like LC-MS, GC–MS, and in situ infrared spectra.

This increase in the free superoxide radicals under visible light irradiation enhanced the degradation efficiency of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 to 94.0 % compared to the N-CDs (90 %) [8], ZIF-8 (82.0 %) [37], ZIF-67 (88.0 %) [78], and ZIF-8@ZIF-67 (60.0 %) [38] as illustrated in Table 2 . Moreover, ZIF-8 showed only photocatalytic activity under UV irradiation. On the other hand, ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 showed photocatalytic activity under simulated sunlight irradiation, which is considered a green source of energy.

ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 is considered as a cost-efficient photocatalyst with higher degradation efficiency as compared to the composite Ag/AgCl/ZIF-67 (90.0 %) without using noble metals. Furthermore, ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67, as compared to ZIF-67 [79], exhibited better water stability, recyclability, and photocatalytic efficacy under visible light irradiation. Finally, ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 maintained the highest degradation efficiency after increasing the dye concentration up to 32.0 ppm.

The synthesized photocatalyst ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized by the encapsulation of N-CQDs in the ZIF-8@ZIF-67 shell for enhanced light absorbance, charge separation, and higher production of . O2 –. The synthesized photocatalyst was characterized with different techniques including TEM, XRD, and FTIR. TEM images showed the synthesized ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 crystals with the characteristic dodecahedron shape with size ∼ 700 nm. Based on the optical studies, the band gap of ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 was found to be 1.77 eV which is less than that of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 (1.88 eV), and N-CQDs (3.16 eV). Thus, ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation of MB under visible and solar light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of MB reaches 94.0 % and 92.0 % under visible and sunlight radiation, while in the dark, the adsorption of MB only reaches 10.0 %. The photodegradation of MB over ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 followed pseudo-first-order kinetics with the rate constant is k = 0.0105 min−1 under visible light irradiation. ZIF-8@N-CQDs/ZIF-67 also showed excellent recycling and high stability after five consecutive cycles. Hence, this work is expected to provide a model for a green, economical, and highly effective photocatalyst for the treatment of various organic pollutants from wastewater.
